# Onboarding Guide — UAV Radar Research Lab

*Auto-generated by curator bot on 2026-04-22. Edit `papers/raw/` to improve this guide.*

# Onboarding Guide: UAV Detection, Identification, and Tracking from Radar Data

---

## 1. Project Overview

Our lab focuses on the detection, identification, and tracking of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) using radar sensor data, with an emphasis on developing and evaluating machine learning algorithms that can operate in real-world, cluttered environments. A central challenge we address is the discrimination problem: small UAVs and birds share overlapping radar cross-section profiles and flight envelopes, making it difficult to reliably tell them apart using simple signal thresholds alone. Our work spans the full pipeline from raw radar signal processing — including range-Doppler cube analysis and micro-Doppler signature extraction — through to kinematic tracking and sequence-based classification using modern deep learning architectures. The primary hardware platform in our lab is the **DAiR tactical multi-mission X-band radar**, a software-defined system with a 320-element digital receive array that produces per-CPI detection plots, full kinematic tracks, and range-Doppler cubes directly usable by our algorithm development pipeline.

Beyond single-sensor classification, the lab is increasingly interested in trajectory-based identification, sim-to-real transfer for overcoming data scarcity, and data-driven dynamic modeling of UAV motion. Because labeled real-world UAV data is expensive and difficult to collect at scale, we invest significant effort in synthetic data generation, pretraining strategies, and generalization-focused architectures. Our work is also beginning to connect with broader situational awareness systems, including multi-sensor fusion and distributed sensing in next-generation networks. Whether your project sits closer to signal processing, machine learning, or systems integration, you will need a working understanding of all three layers of the stack — the radar hardware producing the data, the motion dynamics of the targets, and the learning algorithms making sense of both.

---

## 2. Background Reading

The following six papers form the recommended foundation for a new student. Read them roughly in the order listed, moving from hardware and signal context through classical tracking theory, and then into modern machine learning approaches.

---

### Step 1 — Understand the Hardware Platform

**[0] DAiR Radar – Device and Operation Summary (2026)**

Read this first, before any algorithm paper. Everything else in the lab ultimately connects back to the data this radar produces. Pay close attention to the ICD data model (how detection plots, tracks, and range-Doppler cubes are structured), the concept of Track-While-Search operation, and the GPU-based processing pipeline. Understanding what the radar *outputs* — and the assumptions baked into those outputs — will save you enormous confusion later when you start working with real data.

---

### Step 2 — Classical Tracking Foundation

**[1] Estimating Optimal Tracking Filter Performance for Manned Maneuvering Targets (1970)**

Despite its age, this paper remains one of the clearest derivations of a Kalman filter designed for maneuvering targets. The three-state constant-velocity-baseline model with exponentially correlated acceleration is still the conceptual ancestor of most practical tracking filters used today, including the IMM filters you will encounter in our pipeline. Read this to understand *why* tracking filters are structured the way they are — the trade-offs between process noise assumptions, measurement noise, and data rate are explained with unusual clarity. It also gives you a baseline against which modern neural approaches (see paper [9]) are compared.

---

### Step 3 — The Core Discrimination Problem

**[6] Millimeter Wave Radar Measurements: Distinguishing UAS and Birds Based on 60 GHz micro-Doppler Signatures (2024)**

This paper introduces you to the central discrimination challenge the lab works on: UAVs vs. birds. The physical intuition is essential — rotating propellers and flapping wings produce fundamentally different micro-Doppler modulation patterns, and this paper makes that concrete with real anechoic chamber measurements. Even though it uses a 60 GHz FMCW MIMO system rather than the X-band DAiR radar, the underlying physics and the CNN/ConvLSTM classification methodology are directly relevant. Pay particular attention to the role of *phase* vs. *amplitude* features and how noise affects each.

---

### Step 4 — Learning Dynamics for Classification

**[3] Classification by Prediction for UAV and Bird Recognition Using Direct Phase Imagery from Millimeter-Wave Radar**

This paper introduces a powerful and elegant idea: rather than training a discriminative classifier directly, train *class-conditional predictive models* and classify by which model predicts the next observation most accurately. This "Classification by Prediction" (CBP) framework explicitly learns target motion dynamics, which turns out to be highly informative for separating UAVs from birds. The ConvLSTM instantiation achieves the best accuracy and noise robustness of all tested architectures. You will likely apply or extend variants of this framework in your own work, so understand it deeply.

---

### Step 5 — Trajectory-Based Identification

**[10] UAV Classification with Mamba (2024)**

Where paper [3] operates on radar phase imagery, this paper applies a closely related prediction-error classification paradigm directly to 3D kinematic trajectory sequences. It is a useful complement because it shows the same core idea — classify by comparing generative prediction errors across class-specific models — applied at the tracking layer rather than the raw signal layer. The use of Mamba (a state-space model architecture) is also important to understand because Mamba appears across multiple papers in our knowledge base ([3], [9], [11]) and is increasingly a go-to architecture for sequential radar and tracking data in our lab.

---

### Step 6 — Addressing Data Scarcity

**[9] Synthetic Motion Pretraining Improves Next-Step Real UAV Trajectory Prediction Across Neural Architectures**

One of the most practically important papers for new students to read. Real labeled UAV trajectory data is scarce and expensive to collect; this paper demonstrates a concrete sim-to-real strategy using PX4/Gazebo simulation to pretrain neural sequence models (LSTM, S4, Mamba) before fine-tuning on real data. The 33–35% MSE reduction over real-only baselines is striking, and the finding that *all* synthetically pretrained neural models outperform a classical IMM filter provides strong motivation for the neural approach. Understanding this paper will help you think about how to bootstrap your own experiments when labeled data is limited.

---

Before diving into research tasks, make sure you can explain each of the following concepts at a whiteboard level:
